problem.title: Validate Binary Search Tree
problem.description:
Given the root of a binary tree, determine if it is a valid binary search tree (BST).
                
Definition: A valid BST is defined as follows:
                
The left subtree of a node contains only nodes with keys less than the node's key
The right subtree of a node contains only nodes with keys greater than the node's key
Both the left and right subtrees must also be binary search trees
assumptions: Each node's value must be strictly between its ancestor bounds.

solutions.title: Solution 1 – Inorder Traversal [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: An inorder traversal of a valid BST visits nodes in strictly increasing order; collecting all values and verifying each consecutive pair satisfies this condition confirms BST validity. Any violation — a value not greater than its predecessor — immediately reveals an invalid tree. The key insight is that the sorted-order property of inorder traversal provides a simple, equivalent reformulation of the BST definition.

solutions.title: Solution 2 – Recursive with Range [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: Each node is validated against a (min, max) range that tightens as the recursion descends: going left narrows the upper bound to the parent's value, going right narrows the lower bound. A node is invalid if its value falls outside its allowed range. The key insight is that a single recursive pass with propagated bounds subsumes the BST condition for all ancestors simultaneously, avoiding the need for an extra traversal.

complexity.space: O(h) where h is the height of the tree

solutions.title: Solution 3 – Iterative Inorder [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: An explicit stack simulates inorder traversal without recursion; a 'prev' pointer tracks the last visited node and any out-of-order value immediately returns false. This eliminates recursion stack overhead for very deep trees. The key insight is that iterative inorder traversal with a pointer to the previous value detects the first BST violation as early as possible, without collecting all values first.
solutions.code_python:
# class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0): self.val=val; self.left=self.right=None
from typing import Optional

def is_valid_bst_iterative(root: Optional["TreeNode"]) -> bool:
    stack: list = []
    prev: Optional["TreeNode"] = None
    current = root

    while current or stack:
        while current:
            stack.append(current)
            current = current.left

        current = stack.pop()

        if prev is not None and current.val <= prev.val:
            return False

        prev = current
        current = current.right

    return True
